data.title: U.S. Stock Futures Edge Up After Nasdaq Hits Record High
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff

U.S. stock futures were modestly higher early Friday, with a mixed picture across global stock markets the day after the tech-heavy Nasdaq composite hit a new record high. The dollar remained under pressure and Treasury yields rose after weak debt auctions and rising chances that the Federal Reserve will cut rates in September. President Trump said he would nominate Stephen Miran to fill a vacancy on the Federal Reserve's Board of Governors on a short-term basis. Last year, Miran, the head of the White House Council of Economic Advisers, co-authored a paper arguing for sweeping reforms at the Federal Reserve.

Elsewhere, gold futures rose sharply, building on recent gains, after the U.S. slapped tariffs on 1kg gold bars--a move that will mostly hurt Switzerland, which is also still trying to renegotiate its 39% trade tariff. Benchmark crude-oil prices slipped.

  • U.S. futures for the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.2% after ending Thursday's session lower. Futures for the S&P 500 and Nasdaq were up around 0.2%.
  • The pan-European Stoxx Europe 600 climbed 0.2% in morning trading but major national markets were mixed, with gains in France but Germany's DAX fell.

The U.K's FTSE 100 added 0.2%, with miners leading the pack, buoyed by rising gold prices. Oil and gas companies were mixed across the region amid the slide in oil prices.

  • In Asia, Japan's Nikkei 225 index closed up 1.9%. SoftBank was the biggest gainer during the session, surging 10%, after it reported better-than-expected first-quarter profit on Thursday, chiefly on a recovery in its tech funds business. Hong Kong's Hang Seng declined 1% while South Korea's Kospi ended down 0.5% but gained 2.9% on the week.
  • The dollar fell as investors expect Trump's Fed-board nominee Miran to align with the President's push for lower interest rates. The DXY dollar index against a basket of major currencies was recently down 0.2% to 98.196 after reaching a 10-day low of 97.945 Thursday.
  • Treasury yields continued to nudge higher after Thursday's auction of 30-year notes received weak demand. Analysts say demand for Treasurys has waned after recent weak U.S. jobs data caused yields to fall. The yield on 10-year Treasurys was up 1 basis point to 4.256% early in Europe, while the 30-year yield rose nearly two basis points to 4.830%, Tradeweb data show.
  • In commodities, gold climbed to a fresh record high on Friday following a report that the U.S. slapped tariffs on imports of one-kilo gold bars, a move that threatens to disrupt the global bullion market. In early trade, futures rose 1% to $3,489.50 a troy ounce after reaching a peak of $3,534.20 earlier in the session. The tariffs on one-kilo gold bars--the most common form traded on Comex--deal a fresh blow to top refining hub Switzerland, the Financial Times reported, citing a letter from Customs Border Protection.
  • Oil prices were on track for sharp weekly losses, with President Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin potentially meeting as soon as next week and as U.S. tariffs weigh on the global economic outlook. In early trade in Europe, Brent crude and WTI both edged up around 0.1% to $66.50 and $63.90 a barrel, respectively, but are down 4.6% and 7.7% on the week. A meeting to discuss a Russia-Ukraine peace deal would significantly reduce the geopolitical risk premium and possibly affect Trump's secondary tariffs on India.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: Thyssenkrupp investors to vote on defence spin-off
data.description:
Shareholders in Thyssenkrupp are scheduled to vote on a planned spin-off of its defence division on Friday, as a radical shift in European defence spending has raised expectations its value could surge.

The order book of Thyssenkrupp's TKMS division - which makes submarines, frigates and develops mine-sweeping technologies - has swollen to more than 18 billion euros ($21 billion) from 11.7 billion at the end of September last year.

That has in turn boosted shares of its parent, which have more than doubled year-to-date.

Thyssenkrupp plans to spin off 49% of TKMS to its existing shareholders while keeping the remaining stake.

A vote on the plan will be held later on Friday at a meeting of shareholders, including the Alfried Krupp von Bohlen and Halbach foundation, Thyssenkrupp's largest shareholder. The meeting kicked off at 0800 GMT.

Analysts expect the spin-off and separate listing to take place later this year, although Thyssenkrupp has yet to specify the timing.

Last month, Berlin reached a preliminary agreement with Thyssenkrupp that guarantees it will retain influence at TKMS, as the government seeks to keep a certain level of control over strategic defence assets.

Labour unions have called for Berlin to take a blocking minority in TKMS to ensure stakes in the business do not fall into the wrong hands, but the German government has so far not done so.

Stephanie Schmoliner of labour union IG Metall, who also serves as deputy chairman of TKMS's supervisory board, on Friday repeated these demands, saying a government stake was "essential".

The increase in defence spending comes in response to Russia's invasion of Ukraine, and as U.S. President Donald Trump has called on NATO's European members to raise their military budgets.

($1 = 0.8583 euros)

data.title: Taiwan Trade Surplus Widens Sharply Amid Record Exports
data.description:
Taiwan’s trade surplus surged to USD 14.34 billion in July 2025, nearly tripling from USD 4.90 billion a year earlier.

Exports jumped 42.0% year-on-year to a record USD 56.68 billion, driven mainly by strong growth in information, communication, and audio-video products (+87.1%) and parts of electronic products (+34.1%).

Outbound shipments rose to ASEAN (+71.6%), the US (+62.8%), China & Hong Kong (+23.9%), Europe (+4.1%) and Japan (+0.5%).

Imports rose 20.8% to USD 42.34 billion, mainly due to higher purchases of electronic parts (+41.4%), information and audio-video products (+66.8%), and machinery (+25.8%), despite a decline in mineral products (-11.3%).

Import growth was led by increases from Korea (+41.3%), ASEAN (+32.0%), China & Hong Kong (+16.4%), Japan (+15.4%), the US (+13.5%), and Europe (+1.9%).

For the first seven months, Taiwan’s trade surplus totaled USD 70.05 billion, up 71.0% year-on-year, with exports rising 28.3% and imports increasing 20.5%.

data.title: China stocks end week near 10-month high on strong data
data.description:
China stocks closed slightly down on Friday, but ended the week near their highest level in 10 months, as upbeat economic data lifted sentiment and investors largely looked past U.S. tariff concerns. Hong Kong shares declined on the day.

** China's blue-chip CSI300 Index ended 0.2% lower, while the Shanghai Composite Index was down 0.1%. Hong Kong benchmark Hang Seng slipped 0.9%.

** The Shanghai Composite Index rose to 3,645 points in morning trades, its highest level since October 2024.

** China stocks have steadily climbed this week, supported by upbeat trade and service activity data. For the week, the CSI300 Index gained 1.2%, while the Hang Seng Index rose 1.4%.

** "The market may be underpricing the risk of near-term deterioration in the U.S.-China relationship," said Morgan Stanley strategists led by Laura Wang.

** The strategists urged investors to monitor developments in trade tensions and flagged the upcoming NPC Standing Committee meeting and second-quarter earnings season as potential catalysts for market direction. In the interim, they prefer mainland-listed A-shares over Hong Kong-listed H-shares, citing stronger relative performance during periods of global market volatility.

** U.S. President Donald Trump said on Wednesday that he could announce further tariffs on China over its purchases of Russian oil, depending on the developments in the trade discussions.

** China faces an August 12 deadline to reach a durable tariff agreement with Trump's administration.

** Tech majors traded in Hong Kong were down 1.6%, while materials shares (.HSCIM) were up 2.5%.

** Shares of semiconductors dropped nearly 2%, weighed by an 8% fall in China's largest chipmaker SMIC after it reported weaker-than-expected second-quarter earnings.

data.title: Roche's Obesity Pivot Seems Uncertain Amid Growing Market Concerns — Market Talk
data.description: Roche Holding's pivot into obesity is overshadowed by market uncertainty and competition, Morgan Stanley says. Recent concerns about pricing and access suggest early-stage clinical trials might no longer be a meaningful driver for shares in the Swiss pharmaceutical company, analysts at Morgan Stanley say in a research note. "Compounding, intensifying competition and reimbursement bottlenecks have created a more challenging near-term setup in the U.S. obesity market," they add. The obesity market's long-term potential likely remains intact at about $100 billion sales annually, according to Morgan Stanley. However, Roche's entry into the market and its ability to grab market share are uncertain, the analysts say. Morgan Stanley lowers its estimate for Roche's peak obesity sales to 5.5 billion Swiss francs from 8.5 billion francs. Shares fall 0.7%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: Munich Re Shares Tumble After Revenue View Lowered on Currency Impacts — Update
data.description:
By Elena Vardon

Munich Re shares dropped after the German reinsurer cut its insurance revenue guidance for the year due to business and exchange-rate developments, but backed its profit view as it reported second-quarter results.

The German reinsurer now expects its insurance revenue for 2025 to come in at 62 billion euros ($72.34 billion), down from 64 billion euros previously, it said Friday. The trim is due to lower expectations for insurance revenue in its reinsurance division, which it expects to amount to 40 billion euros against the 42 billion euros previously forecast.

Like many rivals that operate globally, Munich Re faced significant headwinds from currency translation effects in the second quarter, with a weaker U.S. dollar dragging down its revenue. European peers including Allianz, Zurich Insurance, AXA and Scor have in recent days reported second-quarter figures where adverse foreign exchange impacts dented key metrics.

For the second quarter, Munich Re posted 14.775 billion euros in revenue from insurance contracts issued, which was 1.2% lower than a year prior. This missed a 15.5 billion euro estimate taken from a company-compiled consensus.

The stock traded at 559 euros in early European exchanges, shedding 8% and wiping out its gains for the week. "The stock has been very strong coming into these results...partly due to sector rotation," RBC Capital Markets analysts said in a note to clients. Given the recent rally, some profit taking is expected, J.P. Morgan said, adding that Munich Re's investor day in December should act as a catalyst for the stock.

Despite a softer top-line, the Bavarian group also confirmed it made 2.085 billion euros in net profit for the quarter, a 30% increase compared with the same period the previous year on benign major-loss expenditures in its property and casualty reinsurance segment while its life and health reinsurance was hit by an accumulation of individual major losses and its primary insurance subsidiary Ergo met expectations. This beat consensus's 1.62 billion euro figure and was in line with the around 2.1 billion euro result it pre-reported last month.

The contribution of all its business lines "allowed us to mitigate the impact of foreign exchange losses due to the faltering U.S. dollar," said Chief Executive Joachim Wenning, who recently announced he will be retiring by the end of the year and is set to be succeeded by finance chief Christoph Jurecka.

Munich Re said it continues to expect 6 billion euros in net profit for the full year and backed its other targets, it said.

At its July renewals, the group saw a price drop of 2.5% and a volume decrease of 3.2%. Munich Re expects to see a market environment that continues to offer attractive business opportunities at the next round of renewals in January, it added. The lighter renewals with more business not meeting the risk-return requirements also weighs on the stock, UBS analysts said.

Its solvency II ratio--a measure of capital strength--stood at 287% at the end of the period, against consensus' expected 289% ratio, and is largely above its 175% to 220% target range.

Write to Elena Vardon at elena.vardon@wsj.com

data.title: Trade Desk shares slump after slower Q2 revenue growth
data.description:
** Shares of ad-tech firm Trade Desk fall 28.73% to $62.95 in premarket trading

** Co reports sharp slowdown in Q2 rev growth on softer demand for its digital advertising services in the connected TV market

** Q2 rev rose 19%, down from 26% in the year-ago quarter

** Brokerage Piper Sandler trims PT to $64 from $65

** "We worry about competition from Amazon and others, although it's unclear how material this dynamic has been to reported numbers (yet)" - Piper Sandler

** TTD on average is rated "buy" by 40 brokerages; their median PT is $90, as per data compiled by LSEG

** YTD, stock down ~25%

data.title: Bulgaria Industrial Output Posts Steeper Drop
data.description:
Industrial production in Bulgaria fell by 8.2% year-on-year in June 2025, following a downwardly revised 6.0% decline in the previous month.

Output declined further for manufacturing (-7.1% vs -1.3% in May).

Meanwhile, production surged for mining and quarrying (27.5% vs 8.5%), while output contracted at a softer pace for electricity, gas, steam, and air-conditioning supply (-17.6% vs -26.6%).

On a seasonally adjusted monthly basis, industrial activity fell by 1.7% in June, reversing an upwardly revised 0.7% gain in the preceding period.

data.title: Slovakia Trade Surplus Narrows in June
data.description:
Slovakia’s trade surplus narrowed to EUR 334.4 million in June 2025 from EUR 580.7 million in the corresponding month of the previous year, below market expectations of EUR 354 million surplus.

Exports grew by 0.9% year-on-year to EUR 9,157.2 million, mainly driven by a 13% increase in miscellaneous manufactured articles.

Exports rose to both the EU (0.6%) and non-EU countries (2%).

Meanwhile, imports increased by 3.9% year-on-year to EUR 8,822.8 million, mainly contributed by an almost 20% increase in the Miscellaneous industrial products.

Imports from EU countries increased by 4%, while imports from non-EU countries declined by 4%.

For the first half of the year, the country’s trade surplus shrank to EUR 1 billion from EUR 2.8 billion in the same period last year.
